title: Topic 255: The Psychology of Hype Cycles: Anticipation and Disappointment
content:
# Topic 255: The Psychology of Hype Cycles: Anticipation and Disappointment

## Abstract

Humanity, in its infinite wisdom, has perfected two seemingly contradictory arts: the breathless, giddy anticipation of a new product, and the subsequent, bitter complaining when it fails to deliver a transcendent, life-altering experience. This paper delves into the psychological spectacle of the “Hype Cycle,” a recurring tragicomedy in consumer culture. We embark on a rigorous, albeit highly amusing, examination of this rollercoaster of human emotion, from the dizzying heights of the “Peak of Inflated Expectations” to the soul-crushing depths of the “Trough of Disillusionment.” By analyzing a potent cocktail of cognitive biases, social pressures, and the pure, unadulterated pleasure of wanting, we expose the psychological machinery that makes us willing passengers on this ride. Ultimately, this paper serves as a poignant reminder that despite a long and storied history of spectacular derailments, we remain eternally optimistic, ever-ready to leap aboard the next hype train, armed with our wallets and a touching, if profoundly misplaced, sense of hope.

## Introduction

I still remember the Christmas of 1998. The Nintendo 64 was the pinnacle of human achievement, and the imminent release of *The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time* was, to my ten-year-old self, an event of cosmic significance. I had consumed every magazine screenshot, memorized every rumored detail, and dreamed in polygons. The hype was a palpable force, a shimmering aura around a golden cartridge that promised not just a game, but a gateway to a new reality. Then, the day came. The game was… good. It was great, even. But it didn’t, as I had secretly suspected, solve all my problems, make me popular at school, or teach me how to play the actual ocarina. There was a faint, almost imperceptible sigh of disappointment, the quiet deflation of a balloon that had been inflated to the size of a small car. This, in essence, is the hype cycle in miniature: a universal human experience of wanting something so badly that the reality, no matter how good, can feel like a letdown.

This phenomenon, of course, is not limited to the pixelated dreams of children. It is a cornerstone of modern consumerism, a carefully orchestrated and wildly unpredictable dance between marketers and the masses. The brave souls at Gartner, a consulting firm, even attempted to map this madness, giving us the “Gartner Hype Cycle,” a five-stage model that tracks a technology's journey from obscurity to ubiquity. [1] It begins with a “Technology Trigger,” a whisper of a breakthrough. Then comes the “Peak of Inflated Expectations,” a cacophony of breathless media coverage and utopian promises. This is inevitably followed by the “Trough of Disillusionment,” a period of bitter recrimination and buyer’s remorse. For the lucky few, a gentle “Slope of Enlightenment” leads to a final, stable “Plateau of Productivity.” For the rest, it’s a one-way ticket to the bargain bin and a cautionary tale for the ages.

But why do we keep falling for it? What is the psychological engine that powers this perpetual motion machine of hope and despair? This paper argues that the hype cycle is not merely a marketing strategy but a fundamental aspect of our cognitive architecture. It is a story we tell ourselves, a narrative woven from our deepest desires for novelty, connection, and a brief, shining moment of collective excitement. We will dissect this psychological spectacle with the precision of a surgeon and the bewildered amusement of a primatologist observing a particularly baffling ritual. We will explore the siren song of anticipation, the crushing weight of disappointment, and our remarkable ability to forget it all and do it again, bigger and better, the next time around.

## Literature Review

To understand the glorious, chaotic spectacle of the hype cycle, we must first consult the academics and consultants who have bravely attempted to impose order upon it. The most famous of these is the aforementioned Gartner Hype Cycle, a model that bears an uncanny resemblance to the emotional trajectory of a doomed love affair. It begins with the **Technology Trigger**, the first tantalizing glimpse across a crowded room, a moment filled with promise and possibility. This is followed by the **Peak of Inflated Expectations**, the heady, whirlwind romance phase where flaws are invisible and the future seems paved with gold. In this stage, as Gartner notes, a flurry of publicity and success stories (often accompanied by an equal number of failures) creates an intoxicating buzz. [1]

Then comes the inevitable **Trough of Disillusionment**. This is the morning after the first big fight, the shocking discovery that your soulmate snores like a congested walrus. Interest wanes, experiments fail, and the once-bright promise begins to tarnish. It is in this dark valley that many a technological dream goes to die. However, for the resilient few, a path forward emerges: the **Slope of Enlightenment**. This is the “learning to love the imperfections” phase, where the technology’s real-world benefits start to become clear, and second or third-generation products appear, quietly fixing the sins of their predecessors. Finally, if all goes well, the journey culminates in the **Plateau of Productivity**, a state of stable, mainstream adoption, the comfortable domesticity of a technology that has found its place in the world. It’s a compelling narrative, to be sure, but as some critics have pointed out, it’s also a bit of a self-fulfilling prophecy, and a framework that is far from scientific. [1]

But the Gartner model, for all its corporate neatness, only describes the “what,” not the “why.” For that, we must turn to the psychologists, who have long understood that the journey is often more satisfying than the destination. The concept of “consumer anticipation” is key here. Research has shown that the period of waiting for a pleasant experience can, in itself, generate utility. [2] In other words, the daydreams of playing that new video game, the thrill of tracking the package, the endless speculation on fan forums – these are not just idle distractions; they are an integral part of the product’s value. This “anticipation effect” is so powerful that it can create long-lasting positive attitudes, even if the eventual experience is a bit of a dud. [2] We are, it seems, suckers for a good trailer.

This innate love of anticipation is then mercilessly exploited by a rogue’s gallery of cognitive biases. The most infamous of these is **FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out)**, a pervasive social anxiety that turns product launches into cultural events. It’s the nagging feeling that if you’re not part of the conversation, you’re a social pariah. Closely related is **Social Proof**, our tendency to outsource our critical thinking to the crowd. If thousands of people are camping out for a new phone, it must be good, right? The logic is flimsy, but the psychological pull is immense. And let’s not forget our old friend, **Optimism Bias**, the triumph of hope over experience. This is the little voice in our head that whispers, “I know the last ten games in this series were buggy messes, but this one… this one will be different.” It’s a powerful delusion, and the lifeblood of the hype industry.

## Analysis

Having reviewed the scholarly attempts to diagram our collective consumer madness, let us now dissect the beast itself. The hype cycle is a masterclass in psychological manipulation, a grand symphony of marketing genius and cognitive vulnerability. To truly appreciate its artistry, we must examine its two most dramatic movements: the euphoric crescendo of the “Peak of Inflated Expectations” and the crashing, dissonant chord of the “Trough of Disillusionment.”

The ascent to the Peak is a carefully choreographed performance, a modern-day secular revival meeting. It begins with the **Art of the Reveal**. This is not simply an announcement; it is a meticulously crafted piece of theater. The teaser trailer, a cryptic glimpse of a logo, a single, enigmatic screenshot – these are the digital breadcrumbs that lead us down the rabbit hole. The masters of this craft, the Rockstar Games and Apple’s of the world, understand that what they are selling is not a product, but a narrative. They are building a world, and for a limited time, you can pre-order a piece of it. This process is a powerful neurological cocktail. Each new tidbit of information, each beautifully rendered cinematic, triggers a release of dopamine in our brains, the same neurotransmitter associated with pleasure, reward, and addiction. We are, in a very real sense, getting high on our own supply of anticipation.

The descent into the Trough, however, is a far more brutal affair. It is the moment the house lights come up and we realize the magical world we were promised is just a collection of buggy assets and unfulfilled promises. This is where **Cognitive Dissonance** rears its ugly head. We are confronted with two contradictory beliefs: “I am a savvy consumer who makes rational decisions,” and “I just spent $60 on a game that has the physics of a drunken giraffe on a unicycle.” The mental discomfort is palpable. To resolve this, we engage in a series of psychological gymnastics, a process I like to call the **Five Stages of Gamer Grief**. First comes **Denial**: “It’s not a bug, it’s an undocumented feature!” This is quickly followed by **Anger**, a torrent of all-caps fury unleashed upon the unsuspecting moderators of a fan forum. Then, **Bargaining**: “If the developers just release a patch, and another patch, and maybe a complete overhaul of the entire game engine, it will be the masterpiece we were promised!” This inevitably gives way to **Depression**, the sad realization that the game is, and always will be, a bit rubbish. And finally, **Acceptance**: the quiet uninstallation of the game, the solemn vow to never pre-order again, and the immediate pre-ordering of the sequel.

## Real-World Examples

Lest we think this is all just abstract theory and psychological navel-gazing, let us turn to the hallowed halls of hype history, where the ghosts of failed launches and broken promises whisper their cautionary tales. Our first exhibit is a true classic, a story so mythic it involves a secret burial in the desert.

**Case Study 1: *E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial* (1982) - The Original Sin of Hype**

In the early 1980s, Atari was the undisputed king of the video game world, and Steven Spielberg’s *E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial* was the highest-grossing film of all time. The marriage of the two seemed destined for greatness. The hype was immense. This was not just a game; it was a cultural event. Unfortunately, the game itself was developed in a mere five weeks to hit the holiday season. The result was a confusing, unplayable mess that involved falling into pits. A lot of pits. The commercial failure was so catastrophic that it is often cited as a major contributor to the video game industry crash of 1983. The legend culminates in the infamous “Atari video game burial,” where truckloads of unsold *E.T.* cartridges were buried in a New Mexico landfill, a perfect, poignant symbol of a hype cycle crashing and burning. [3]

**Case Study 2: *Cyberpunk 2077* (2020) - A Cautionary Tale for the Modern Age**

Fast forward nearly four decades, and we find a new generation of gamers ready to be hurt again. *Cyberpunk 2077*, from the acclaimed developers of *The Witcher 3*, was the subject of an eight-year hype campaign of unprecedented intensity. The marketing was a masterpiece, culminating in the now-legendary E3 appearance of Keanu Reeves, who declared the game “breathtaking.” The anticipation was at a fever pitch. The reality, at least at launch, was a technical disaster. The game was riddled with bugs, glitches, and performance issues so severe that Sony took the unprecedented step of removing it from the PlayStation Store. [3] The backlash was swift and brutal. The story of *Cyberpunk 2077* is a modern parable of hype, a testament to the fact that no amount of marketing savvy or celebrity endorsement can save a product that is fundamentally broken. While the developers have since worked tirelessly to fix the game, its launch remains a stark reminder of the dangers of believing your own hype.

These are but two of the most egregious examples. The annals of consumer history are littered with the corpses of overhyped products, from the Segway, which was supposed to revolutionize transportation, to the Fyre Festival, a luxury music festival that turned out to be a cheese sandwich in a disaster zone. The pattern is always the same: a brilliant, seductive promise, a period of frenzied, collective excitement, and a cold, hard landing in the unforgiving terrain of reality.

## Discussion

So, what have we learned from this whirlwind tour of our own consumerist follies, besides the sage advice to never, ever pre-order a video game? The hype cycle, in all its glory and absurdity, offers a fascinating window into the modern human psyche. It suggests that we are not the rational, utility-maximizing creatures that classical economics would have us believe. Instead, we are narrative-driven, emotional beings, easily swayed by a good story and a shared sense of excitement. The hype cycle is, in many ways, a form of collective storytelling, a myth we co-create with marketers. For a brief, shining moment, we are all part of something bigger than ourselves, united in our anticipation of a brighter, more exciting future, delivered in a sleek, shrink-wrapped package.

This, of course, raises some thorny ethical questions. Is it morally defensible for a company to knowingly cultivate expectations that its product cannot possibly meet? Where is the line between enthusiastic marketing and outright deception? The story of *Cyberpunk 2077*, with its carefully curated demos that bore little resemblance to the final product, suggests that this line is often blurry and easily crossed. And yet, can we, the consumers, truly absolve ourselves of responsibility? We are not passive victims in this drama; we are active participants. We are the ones who share the trailers, who engage in the endless speculation, who build the very echo chambers that amplify the hype to deafening levels. Perhaps, on some level, we enjoy the spectacle. The drama, the community, the shared emotional journey – maybe these are the real products we are consuming. The game, the phone, the gadget – perhaps they are just expensive souvenirs of the experience.

Looking to the future, it is clear that the hype cycle is not going anywhere. In an age of social media, where trends can emerge and vanish in the blink of an eye, the machinery of hype is more powerful than ever. Influencer marketing, viral campaigns, and AI-driven advertising will only make the process more sophisticated, more personalized, and more irresistible. The fundamental psychological principles, however, will remain the same. We are, and will likely always be, creatures of hope, forever chasing the next big thing, the next promise of a better, more exciting life. The hype cycle is not a bug in the system; it is a feature of the human condition.

## Conclusion

We have journeyed through the dizzying highs and crushing lows of the hype cycle, a rollercoaster of our own making. We have seen how our innate love of anticipation, amplified by a potent cocktail of cognitive biases and marketing wizardry, sets us up for a fall. We have mourned at the graves of failed products and marveled at our own capacity to forget, forgive, and do it all over again. The hype cycle, it seems, is less a cycle and more a spiral, an endless, looping dance of hope and disappointment that is as predictable as it is entertaining.

Ultimately, the psychology of hype is the psychology of hope itself. It is the belief that the next thing will be the one, the product that will finally fill the void, the experience that will make us whole. It is a deeply human, and deeply flawed, impulse. And so, as we conclude this study, we can be certain of only one thing: somewhere, right now, a marketing team is putting the finishing touches on a teaser trailer for a product that does not yet exist. And somewhere, a consumer, bruised but not broken by the last great disappointment, is leaning forward, their eyes wide with a familiar, unshakeable sense of optimism, whispering to themselves the five most dangerous words in the English language: “This time will be different.”
references:
[1] Wikipedia. (2024). *Gartner hype cycle*. Retrieved from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gartner_hype_cycle

[2] Vichiengior, T., Ackermann, C. L., & Palmer, A. (2025). When waiting makes sense: How consumer anticipation affects later evaluations. *International Journal of Research in Marketing*, *42*(1), 74-94. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijresmar.2024.06.001

[3] Regaudie, T. (2022, April 6). The hype-cycle history of gaming: From E.T. to Cyberpunk 2077. *#paid*. Retrieved from https://hashtagpaid.com/banknotes/the-hype-cycle-history-of-gaming
